body: The emissions on transport versus diet are of course going to be vastly different in Europe than in other parts of the world. Much of Europe is capable of supporting agriculture and efficient railways fed by nuclear energy provide for minimal carbon impact during transport. The reason I explicitly stated free range or wild hunted animals is because they eat plants that humans wouldn't be capable of eating. At the very least not as part of a balanced diet. I fully support a reduction in meat consumption and efforts to plant more trees. No idea where you got the idea that I think it's a negative. I was simply pointing out the reality that planting trees alone won't fix the issues we currently face. Wildfires are just one of the reasons that it isn't a viable long term solution. Methane production from factory farm animals is the result of the diet they are fed and is absolutely one of the things that should be regulated. The idea that we currently grow enough food for everyone to survive also simply isn't accurate. It doesn't account for the millions of people who can't live off a soy based diet or the multitude of health conditions that would be incompatible with a vegan diet. I never made the claim a single thing would fix climate change. I would agree reducing meat consumption and regulating the industry would absolutely help. Eliminating meat would lead to far more problems than it would solve.

body: Elon Musk can be quite a jerk sometimes but he generally spends his money on tech that helps the world (while also making him more money). And usually the tech is meant to help the world on the large scale rather than helping people directly like what charities are doing. Major threat to humanity in the short-term: Climate change. Major technology necessary to fight climate change that was still in its infancy in the early 2000s: Electric cars. Problem: EVs are seen as uncool and only ecohippies want them. Solution: Make cool EVs that are desirable even for people who don't care about the environment. Therefore, Tesla. Also makes a ton of money being a trillion dollar company. How likely is it for humanity to survive long-term (tens of thousands of years)? Major global disasters are possible and even likely, a humanity that is spread out across the stars is less likely to be destroyed by one big disaster than a humanity that is on one planet only. What's needed to settle on other planets? Much, much better rockets. Therefore, SpaceX. Also makes a ton of money launching satellites for customers with their somewhat better rockets while they're developing the much better rockets. Internet is super important and not available for a lot of people (and their much better rockets need something to do to be profitable while they develop the much, much better rockets) and internet from space is financially viable with their much better rockets, therefore Starlink. Also makes a ton of money selling internet services, and comes in super useful in emergencies like natural disasters and wars.
